parts.content: Analyze the specific roles played by cholesterol and glycoproteins in maintaining the integrity and function of the plasma membrane.
parts.markscheme:
- Cholesterol is an amphipathic molecule that embeds within the hydrophobic tail region of the phospholipid bilayer <marks>1 mark</marks>
- It regulates membrane fluidity by restricting the movement of phospholipids at high temperatures <marks>1 mark</marks>
- It prevents the membrane from solidifying/crystallizing at low temperatures by disrupting the regular packing of hydrocarbon chains <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Glycoproteins consist of carbohydrate chains covalently bonded to proteins on the extracellular surface <marks>1 mark</marks>
- They serve as cell-to-cell recognition markers (e.g., identifying self vs. non-self in the immune system) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- They act as receptors for chemical signals such as hormones or neurotransmitters <marks>1 mark</marks>
- They are involved in cell adhesion, helping cells stick together to form tissues <marks>1 mark</marks>
<note>Award maximum 4 marks</note>

parts.content: Detail the sequence of events in a single cycle of the sodium-potassium ($$Na^+/K^+$$) pump and explain how this contributes to the cell's electrochemical gradient.
parts.markscheme:
- The pump is an integral transmembrane protein with high affinity for sodium ions in its internal configuration <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Three sodium ions ($$3Na^+$$) bind to specific binding sites on the intracellular side of the pump <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The binding of $$Na^+$$ triggers the hydrolysis of $$ATP$$, which phosphorylates the pump (attaches a phosphate group) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Phosphorylation causes a conformational change (shape change) in the protein, translocating the $$Na^+$$ across the membrane <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The $$3Na^+$$ ions are released to the extracellular fluid, and the pump now has a high affinity for potassium ions <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Two potassium ions ($$2K^+$$) bind to the pump from the extracellular side, triggering the release of the phosphate group <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Dephosphorylation causes the pump to return to its original conformation, releasing the $$2K^+$$ into the cytoplasm <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Because more positive charge is moved out ($$3Na^+$$) than in ($$2K^+$$), the pump is electrogenic and establishes a negative internal membrane potential <marks>1 mark</marks>
<note>Award maximum 7 marks</note>

parts.content: Explain how the fluidity of the phospholipid bilayer enables the processes of endocytosis and exocytosis.
parts.markscheme:
- The fluid nature of the membrane is due to the weak hydrophobic interactions between the fatty acid tails, allowing phospholipids to move laterally <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This flexibility allows the plasma membrane to change shape and invaginate to form vesicles during endocytosis <marks>1 mark</marks>
- During exocytosis, the phospholipid bilayers of the secretory vesicle and the plasma membrane can merge/fuse because they are both fluid <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Fusion is followed by the spontaneous rearrangement of the bilayer to become continuous, releasing the vesicle's contents <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Fluidity ensures that the membrane can break and reform (re-seal) without losing its integrity or leaking cytoplasm <marks>1 mark</marks>
<note>Award maximum 4 marks</note>

parts.content: Describe how temperature and pH can affect the permeability of cell membranes.
parts.markscheme:
- High temperatures increase membrane fluidity, making it more permeable. [1]
- Low temperatures reduce fluidity, making the membrane more rigid and less permeable. [1]
- Extreme pH can denature membrane proteins and disrupt lipid interactions, increasing permeability. [1]
- Both temperature and pH must be optimal for maintaining proper membrane function. [1]

Note: Award marks for clear understanding of how each factor affects membrane structure and function.

steps.title: Analyze the question requirements
steps.content:
This question asks us to describe how **temperature** and **pH** influence the permeability of cell membranes. To earn full marks, we need to address both high and low extremes for temperature and explain the impact of pH on the membrane's components, such as proteins and lipids.

This topic is covered in **Topic B2.1: Membranes and membrane transport** and **Topic B1.2: Proteins**. Understanding the fluid mosaic model is essential here.

highlights.text: Describe how temperature and pH can affect the permeability of cell membranes.

steps.title: High temperature and fluidity
steps.content:
When temperature increases, the kinetic energy of the phospholipid molecules in the bilayer also increases. This leads to more movement and a higher state of **fluidity**.

As the phospholipids move further apart, the membrane becomes more porous or "leaky," which **increases its permeability**. This allows substances to pass through more easily than they would at lower temperatures.

highlights.text: High temperatures increase membrane fluidity, making it more permeable.

steps.title: Low temperature and rigidity
steps.content:
Conversely, at low temperatures, phospholipids have less kinetic energy and pack more closely together. This makes the membrane more **rigid** and less fluid.

In this state, the membrane acts as a more effective barrier, which **reduces its permeability**. The molecules are so tightly packed that it is difficult for substances to diffuse across the bilayer.

highlights.text: Low temperatures reduce fluidity, making the membrane more rigid and less permeable.

steps.title: Extreme pH and denaturation
steps.content:
Cell membranes contain many integral and peripheral proteins (like channels and pumps). Extreme pH levels (either very acidic or very alkaline) can disrupt the ionic and hydrogen bonds holding these proteins in their specific shapes.

This process, called **denaturation**, causes the proteins to lose their function and can create "holes" in the membrane. Additionally, extreme pH can disrupt the interactions between the polar heads of phospholipids, overall **increasing permeability** as the membrane structure begins to break down.

highlights.text: Extreme pH can denature membrane proteins and disrupt lipid interactions, increasing permeability.

steps.title: Identify the optimal conditions
steps.content:
For a cell to survive and maintain homeostasis, its membrane must be semi-permeable—selective enough to keep the "good stuff" in and "bad stuff" out. 

Therefore, both **temperature and pH must remain within an optimal range**. If conditions shift too far from this optimum, the membrane loses its ability to regulate transport effectively, which can lead to cell death.

highlights.text: Both temperature and pH must be optimal for maintaining proper membrane function.

steps.title: The Fluid Mosaic Model
steps.content: To understand permeability, we first look at the membrane's structure. According to the **Fluid Mosaic Model**, the membrane is a double layer of phospholipids with proteins embedded within it. Permeability refers to how easily molecules can pass through this molecular barrier. Both temperature and pH affect the stability and movement of these specific components.

steps.title: High Temperature and Fluidity
steps.content:
As temperature increases, phospholipids gain more **kinetic energy**. This causes them to vibrate and move more rapidly within the bilayer. 

This increased movement leads to **increased membrane fluidity**, as the phospholipids are no longer packed tightly together. This creates small gaps in the membrane, allowing substances to leak through more easily, which increases permeability.

highlights.text: High temperatures increase membrane fluidity, making it more permeable.

steps.title: Low Temperature and Rigidity
steps.content:
Conversely, at low temperatures, phospholipids have very little kinetic energy. They pack closely together, making the membrane **rigid** or even crystalline. 

Because the molecules are so tightly packed, there is very little space for substances to move through the bilayer, which significantly **reduces permeability**.

highlights.text: Low temperatures reduce fluidity, making the membrane more rigid and less permeable.

steps.title: pH and Protein Denaturation
steps.content:
While temperature primarily affects the lipids, pH significantly impacts **integral membrane proteins**. These proteins often act as gates or channels for transport. 

Changes in pH (extreme acidity or alkalinity) disrupt the hydrogen and ionic bonds that hold a protein's tertiary structure together. This causes the protein to **denature** (lose its shape). When these proteins deform, they can leave "holes" or gaps in the membrane structure, which disrupts the selective barrier and increases permeability.

highlights.text: Extreme pH can denature membrane proteins and disrupt lipid interactions, increasing permeability.

steps.title: Maintaining Membrane Integrity
steps.content: In summary, for a cell to function correctly, it must maintain a stable internal environment. If temperature or pH fluctuates too far from the **optimum**, the physical structure of the membrane (the lipids and proteins) fails, and the cell can no longer regulate what enters or leaves.

highlights.text: Both temperature and pH must be optimal for maintaining proper membrane function.

parts.content: Explain the role of cholesterol in maintaining the fluidity of the membrane.
parts.markscheme:
- Cholesterol is interspersed between fatty acid tails of the phospholipid bilayer <marks>[1]</marks>

- Prevents fatty acid chains from packing too closely together in cold temperatures, maintaining membrane fluidity <marks>[1]</marks>

- Restrains movement of fatty acid chains in high temperatures, preventing excessive fluidity <marks>[1]</marks>

- Ensures the membrane remains flexible and functional across temperature variations <marks>[1]</marks>

[Total: 4 marks]

steps.title: Identify cholesterol's position
steps.content:
To understand how cholesterol works, we first look at where it is located. In animal cell membranes, cholesterol is an amphipathic molecule found **interspersed between the fatty acid tails** of the phospholipid bilayer. 

Its hydrophobic steroid rings sit among the hydrocarbon tails, while its tiny hydrophilic hydroxyl (-OH) group aligns with the phosphate heads of the phospholipids.

highlights.text: Cholesterol is interspersed between fatty acid tails of the phospholipid bilayer

steps.title: Mechanism at low temperatures
steps.content:
In cold environments, phospholipids naturally lose kinetic energy and tend to cluster together, which could cause the membrane to solidify into a rigid, crystalline state. 

Cholesterol acts as a physical spacer. By being wedged between the tails, it **prevents the fatty acid chains from packing too closely together**, which maintains membrane fluidity even when it's cold.

highlights.text: Prevents fatty acid chains from packing too closely together in cold temperatures, maintaining membrane fluidity

steps.title: Mechanism at high temperatures
steps.content:
Conversely, at high temperatures, phospholipids move more rapidly, which can make the membrane too liquid or "leaky." 

Cholesterol's rigid ring structure helps **restrain the movement of the fatty acid chains**. It essentially pulls the tails together, increasing the membrane's stability and preventing it from becoming excessively fluid.

highlights.text: Restrains movement of fatty acid chains in high temperatures, preventing excessive fluidity

steps.title: Summarize the buffering effect
steps.content:
Because cholesterol has these two opposing effects, it is often called a "fluidity buffer." Its presence **ensures the membrane remains flexible and functional** across a wide range of temperature variations. 

This is vital for cell survival, as the membrane must be fluid enough for proteins to move and for substances to be transported, but stable enough to maintain the cell's integrity.

highlights.text: Ensures the membrane remains flexible and functional across temperature variations

questions.specification:
A scientist compared the uptake kinetics of two solutes into a cell. The results are shown in the graph below (axes in arbitrary units):

![Graph of solute uptake](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-physics-new-e63ccfd7-db80-4a27-86ab-8e02823e25fb-1771929956947/d4409903-b8c4-4521-b90a-5d5cf2d431b2.jpeg)

Which explanation accounts for the shape of the curve for Line Y at higher external solute concentrations?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B - Form and Function > B2.1 Membranes and membrane transport > B2.1.6 Channel proteins for facilitated diffusion
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B2.1.6 Channel proteins for facilitated diffusion
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The hyperbolic curve of Line Y indicates saturation kinetics, which occurs in facilitated diffusion when all available transport channel proteins are occupied at high solute concentrations.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B - Form and Function > B2.1 Membranes and membrane transport > B2.1.7 Pump proteins for active transport
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B2.1.7 Pump proteins for active transport
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Active transport via pump proteins also exhibits saturation kinetics, making it a plausible explanation for the plateau observed in Line Y at higher concentrations.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B - Form and Function > B2.1 Membranes and membrane transport > B2.1.3 Simple diffusion across membranes
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B2.1.3 Simple diffusion across membranes
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Line X in the graph represents simple diffusion, which is included to provide a baseline comparison showing that the rate of simple diffusion remains proportional to the concentration gradient.

options.content: The internal and external concentrations of solute $$Y$$ have reached equilibrium.
options.markscheme: Equilibrium would reduce net uptake toward zero rather than produce a plateau at a positive maximum rate.

options.content: The transport proteins involved in the movement of solute $$Y$$ have reached their maximum capacity.
options.markscheme: A plateau at high external concentration indicates saturation of transport proteins, limiting the maximum uptake rate.

options.content: The phospholipid bilayer has become less permeable to solute $$Y$$ at higher concentrations.
options.markscheme: Permeability of the bilayer does not typically decrease simply because external solute concentration increases; the curve suggests carrier saturation instead.

options.content: The cell has run out of ATP, preventing further transport of solute $$Y$$.
options.markscheme: ATP depletion would not specifically cause a concentration-dependent plateau characteristic of saturable transport; uptake would be broadly impaired.

parts.content: Describe the process of osmosis and how it affects cell volume.
parts.markscheme:
- Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules across a selectively permeable membrane <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Net movement of water is down a water potential gradient (from higher water potential / lower solute concentration to lower water potential / higher solute concentration) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- In a hypotonic solution, water enters the cell causing the cell to swell (animal cells may lyse; plant cells become turgid) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- In a hypertonic solution, water leaves the cell causing the cell to shrink (animal cells crenate; plant cells plasmolyse) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how the sodium–potassium pump helps to establish and maintain the resting membrane potential.
parts.markscheme:
- Sodium–potassium pump is a transmembrane protein in the plasma membrane of neurons <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Uses energy from ATP (ATP hydrolysis) to move ions against their concentration gradients (active transport) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Transports 3 $\text{Na}^+$ ions out of the cell for every 2 $\text{K}^+$ ions moved into the cell <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Net movement of one positive charge out per cycle makes the inside relatively more negative (electrogenic) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Contributes to/maintains the ion concentration gradients (high $\text{Na}^+$ outside, high $\text{K}^+$ inside) needed for resting potential <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Resting membrane potential is maintained by combined effect of pump and selective permeability to $\text{K}^+$ (leak channels) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how voltage-gated ion channels change the membrane potential during the generation of an action potential.
parts.markscheme:
- When threshold is reached, voltage-gated $\text{Na}^+$ channels open causing $\text{Na}^+$ to diffuse into the axon down its electrochemical gradient <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Influx of $\text{Na}^+$ causes depolarization (membrane potential becomes less negative/positive inside) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Depolarization triggers more voltage-gated $\text{Na}^+$ channels to open (positive feedback) producing a rapid rising phase <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Voltage-gated $\text{Na}^+$ channels inactivate/close after a short time, stopping $\text{Na}^+$ influx <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Voltage-gated $\text{K}^+$ channels open (often delayed) allowing $\text{K}^+$ to diffuse out, leading to repolarization <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Continued $\text{K}^+$ efflux can cause hyperpolarization (undershoot) before returning to resting potential <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how nerve impulses are transmitted along a neuron and across a synapse, including the roles of myelination and neurotransmitters.
parts.markscheme:
- Action potential propagates along the axon as local currents depolarize adjacent membrane to threshold, opening voltage-gated $\text{Na}^+$ channels sequentially <marks>1 mark</marks>
- One-way transmission is ensured by the refractory period due to inactivated $\text{Na}^+$ channels preventing immediate re-opening behind the impulse <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Myelin sheath acts as an electrical insulator reducing ion leakage across the membrane <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Voltage-gated channels are concentrated at nodes of Ranvier so depolarization occurs mainly at nodes (saltatory conduction) increasing conduction speed <marks>1 mark</marks>
- At a presynaptic terminal, arrival of an action potential opens voltage-gated $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ channels causing $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ influx <marks>1 mark</marks>
- $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ triggers synaptic vesicles to fuse with the presynaptic membrane and release neurotransmitter by exocytosis <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Neurotransmitter diffuses across the synaptic cleft and binds to receptors on the postsynaptic membrane, opening ligand-gated ion channels <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Ion movement (e.g., $\text{Na}^+$ influx) produces a postsynaptic potential; if threshold is reached a new action potential is generated <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Neurotransmitter is removed (enzymatic breakdown, reuptake into presynaptic neuron, or diffusion away) to stop stimulation and allow repeated signalling <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Synaptic transmission is unidirectional because neurotransmitter is released from presynaptic vesicles and receptors are on the postsynaptic membrane <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.markscheme:

steps.title: Introduction to Exocytosis
steps.content: Exocytosis is a form of **bulk transport** that moves large amounts of material out of the cell. This process is made possible by the fluidity of the phospholipid bilayer, which allows membranes to break and reform easily. This concept is covered in **Topic B2.1: Membranes and membrane transport**.

steps.title: Vesicle Movement and Fusion
steps.content:
The first stage shown in the diagram involves a **vesicle** (a small membrane-bound sac) moving through the cytoplasm toward the cell boundary. Because both the vesicle and the cell's outer boundary are made of phospholipids, the **vesicle fuses with the plasma membrane**.

This fusion essentially merges the two membranes into one continuous surface.

highlights.text: Vesicles fuse with the plasma membrane

steps.title: Release of Contents
steps.content: Once fusion occurs, the pore of the vesicle opens toward the outside of the cell. The **contents of the vesicle are then released into the extracellular space**. This allows the cell to export molecules that are too large to pass through membrane proteins or the lipid bilayer itself.

highlights.text: Contents of the vesicle are released into the extracellular space

steps.title: Biological Significance and Examples
steps.content:
In a biological context, exocytosis is a vital secretory pathway. For example, it is used by neurons to release **neurotransmitters** into a synapse to send signals, or by endocrine cells to secrete **hormones** (like insulin) into the bloodstream.

To earn full marks, you must mention that this process is important for the **secretion of materials like hormones and neurotransmitters**.

highlights.text: Process is important for secretion of materials like hormones and neurotransmitters
